label: Fat Acceptance Movement
summary: Social movement advocating for dignity and reduced discrimination toward people of larger body size. Contains legitimate advocacy and ideological capture in tension.
contemporary_relevance: Continues to shape healthcare policy debates and body image discourse. Tension between anti-discrimination advocacy and medical guidance remains unresolved in clinical practice.

label: Incel Community
summary: Online community of self-identified involuntary celibates. Characterized by external attribution of failure, misogyny, and ideological frameworks that prevent self-examination.
contemporary_relevance: Has radicalized into a terrorism-linked subculture. Multiple mass casualty events have been attributed to incel ideology since 2014, with ongoing recruitment through social media platforms.

label: Brony Fandom
summary: Adult male fanbase of My Little Pony: Friendship is Magic. Characterized by intense identity attachment to children's media, often associated with social alienation and identity displacement.

label: Furry Fandom
summary: Subculture centered on anthropomorphic animal characters. Notable for fursona identity displacement, high rate of sexual content, and documented community predation issues.

label: Chronic Illness Online Communities
summary: Online forums organized around shared medical conditions. Can provide genuine support but frequently develop identity reward structures that discourage recovery and amplify worst-case narratives.

label: Milgram Obedience Experiments (1961)
summary: Stanley Milgram's Yale experiments in which ordinary American participants were instructed by an authority figure to administer increasingly powerful electric shocks to a stranger — with 65% willing to administer the maximum 450-volt shock despite the victim's apparent agony, as long as the authority figure said 'please continue.' Milgram designed the experiments to understand how ordinary Germans could have participated in the Holocaust. The results demonstrated empirically that ordinary people will commit apparent atrocities under authority pressure.

label: Stanford Prison Experiment (1971)
summary: Philip Zimbardo's simulated prison experiment at Stanford in which student volunteers were randomly assigned as 'guards' or 'prisoners' — producing sadistic guard behavior and prisoner psychological breakdown within six days, forcing the experiment's early termination. The experiment demonstrated that arbitrary role assignments and institutional contexts can rapidly produce cruelty in ordinary people, providing experimental support for situational explanations of atrocity over dispositional ones.

label: Confucianism
summary: The philosophical and ethical tradition derived from Confucius' teachings, developed over centuries by Mencius (human nature is inherently good), Xunzi (human nature requires ritual to be shaped), Dong Zhongshu (who made Confucianism China's official state ideology in the Han dynasty), and Zhu Xi (Neo-Confucian synthesis with Buddhist and Taoist elements). Confucianism shaped Chinese governance, education, family structure, and social ethics for two millennia — and through China's cultural influence, much of Korea, Japan, and Vietnam as well.

label: Rabbinic Judaism
summary: The form of Judaism that emerged after the destruction of the Second Temple (70 CE), developed by the Pharisaic movement under the leadership of rabbis. With sacrifice impossible, rabbinic Judaism substituted prayer, Torah study, and ethical observance as the primary religious acts. The rabbis compiled the Mishnah (c.200 CE) and Talmud (c.500 CE), creating a portable, text-based religion that could survive and maintain coherent identity without a homeland. Rabbinic Judaism is the ancestor of all modern forms of Judaism — Orthodox, Conservative, Reform — and its invention of diaspora religion as normative rather than catastrophic was one of the most consequential acts of religious reconstitution in history.

label: Sigmund Freud
summary: Austrian neurologist (1856–1939) who founded psychoanalysis — the theory that unconscious mental processes drive behavior, that early childhood experiences shape adult psychology, and that psychological symptoms are meaningful expressions of repressed conflict rather than random neural events. Freud concepts — the unconscious, repression, the Oedipus complex, id/ego/superego, defense mechanisms — entered the general culture so thoroughly that they shape how educated people in the West think about themselves. His scientific status is contested: many specific Freudian claims are not falsifiable by empirical evidence, and his case studies (Dora, Wolf Man, Little Hans) are now read as more revealing about Freud than about his patients. His influence on art, literature, and cultural analysis was enormous; the Frankfurt School combined Marx and Freud to analyze the psychological dimensions of capitalist ideology.

label: Stanley Milgram
summary: American social psychologist (1933–1984) whose obedience experiments (1961–1963) produced the most important empirical finding in the history of social psychology: that ordinary Americans would administer what they believed were potentially lethal electric shocks to innocent strangers when instructed by an authority figure. Approximately 65% of participants in Milgram original study delivered the maximum 450-volt shock despite the victim's apparent agony. Milgram designed the experiments specifically to understand how ordinary Germans had participated in the Holocaust, asking whether something cultural or situational explained Nazi perpetration. His answer — that situational authority, not character, is the primary determinant — was deeply disturbing and has been confirmed by replications in multiple countries. The experiments raised major ethical questions about psychological research methodology and have not been replicated at full intensity since.

label: Jordan Peterson
summary: Canadian clinical psychologist who became a global media figure following his 2016 refusal to use Bill C-16 pronouns. His YouTube lectures on Jungian archetypes, biblical symbolism, and personal responsibility attracted millions. His work is contested: supporters see practical self-improvement philosophy; critics see a palatable gateway to far-right gender and racial hierarchies. His personal struggles with benzodiazepine dependency complicated his self-help authority.

label: Red Pill / Manosphere
summary: Loosely connected online communities (Reddit r/TheRedPill, 4chan) that claim to expose "uncomfortable truths" about gender, sexual dynamics, and social hierarchy hidden by feminism and mainstream culture. Ranges from self-improvement advice (lifting, stoicism) to misogynistic theory of female nature and "hypergamy." Gateway to more extreme movements including incels and far-right radicalisation. Named from The Matrix: the red pill as truth vs. comfortable blue-pill illusion.

label: MGTOW (Men Going Their Own Way)
summary: Online movement advocating male withdrawal from romantic relationships with women, framed as escaping feminist oppression. Ranges from self-sufficiency philosophy (MGTOW "monk mode") to deep misogyny. Grew on YouTube and Reddit, partially overlaps with incel and red pill communities. Represents a response to perceived loss of traditional male social roles in changed economic and cultural conditions.

label: Pick-Up Artist (PUA) Community
summary: Subculture of men who use scripted techniques to achieve sexual success. Systematised by Neil Strauss's The Game (2005) and community figures like Mystery. Conceptualises seduction as a manipulative skill set using negging, false scarcity, and social proof. Preceded the broader manosphere and contributed its framework of women as targets of competitive male strategy. Critics argue it normalises coercion; defenders frame it as honest self-improvement.

label: Self-Help Industry
summary: A $11+ billion US industry of books, seminars, podcasts, and coaching promising personal transformation. Structural features: advice that is unfalsifiable (any failure is the reader's fault), perpetual consumption (each book promises what the last didn't deliver), and pathologising normal life struggles as individual deficiencies. The industry monetises precarity by reframing systemic problems (job insecurity, social isolation) as personal failures requiring personal solutions.

label: Social Media Addiction & Design
summary: Social media platforms are deliberately engineered for compulsive use: variable ratio reinforcement (the same mechanism as slot machines) via unpredictable likes and notifications, infinite scroll eliminating natural stopping points, and social comparison metrics (follower counts, likes) that exploit status anxiety. Internal research at Facebook (leaked by Frances Haugen, 2021) showed awareness of harm to teen girls' body image. The business model requires attention maximisation regardless of psychological cost.

label: Positive Psychology Movement
summary: Academic movement (Martin Seligman, Mihaly Csikszentmihalyi, from ~2000) studying wellbeing, flourishing, and strengths rather than pathology. Influenced corporate wellness programmes, positive thinking culture, and "happiness science." Critics argue it overclaims research findings, is deployed by corporations to manage workers rather than address working conditions, and its pop-psychology form blames depression on insufficient positivity.

label: Cult Dynamics & Thought Reform
summary: Robert Lifton's criteria for thought reform environments: milieu control, mystical manipulation, demand for purity, confession, sacred science, loading the language, doctrine over person, dispensing of existence. These patterns appear in religious cults, MLM organisations, political movements, and certain corporate cultures. Steven Hassan's BITE model (Behaviour, Information, Thought, Emotional control) provides a framework for identifying coercive control in any organisation.

label: Trauma Bonding & Stockholm Syndrome
summary: The psychological attachment that forms between a victim and abuser, reinforced by cycles of abuse and intermittent reward. Patrick Carnes identified this in domestic abuse; "Stockholm syndrome" (1973 bank siege) named the phenomenon in hostage situations. The same mechanism operates in high-control groups, coercive romantic relationships, and certain institutional environments. Intermittent reinforcement is more powerful than consistent reward in creating compulsive attachment.

label: Narcissistic Abuse / Dark Triad
summary: The "dark triad" (narcissism, Machiavellianism, psychopathy) and "narcissistic abuse" as popular psychological frameworks became culturally dominant in the 2010s through social media, online communities, and self-help literature. The frameworks offer a vocabulary for interpersonal harm but also enabled pathologizing of ordinary relationship conflict, political opponents, and public figures. The dark triad research is legitimate; the popular "narcissistic abuse survivor" community represents its overapplication to non-clinical contexts.

label: Recovered Memory Therapy & Satanic Panic
summary: The recovered memory therapy movement (1980s–90s) and its extreme form, Satanic Panic (ritual abuse allegations at daycare centers), demonstrated how therapeutic techniques could create false memories of abuse at scale. Therapists using hypnosis, guided imagery, and "body memories" produced hundreds of false accusations; the McMartin Preschool case, Paul Ingram case, and similar prosecutions destroyed lives based on iatrogenic (therapist-induced) memories. The scandal produced major advances in memory science (Elizabeth Loftus) and standards for forensic interviewing.

label: Attachment Theory
summary: Attachment theory (John Bowlby, Mary Ainsworth) established that early caregiver relationships form internal working models that shape all subsequent relationships. The Strange Situation procedure identified secure, anxious-ambivalent, avoidant, and disorganized attachment styles, now widely applied to adult relationships. Attachment theory bridges developmental psychology, clinical therapy, and evolutionary biology, and has been extended to explain cult dynamics, trauma bonding, political leadership followership, and online community formation.

label: Online Radicalization Pathways
summary: Online radicalization pathways — the algorithmic and social mechanisms by which people move from mainstream content to extremist ideology via recommendation engines, community escalation, and identity capture — have produced domestic terrorism, incel violence, white nationalist movements, and QAnon. The "rabbit hole" metaphor describes how recommendation algorithms optimize for engagement by escalating emotional intensity, leading users through progressively more extreme content. The radicalization pipeline is now a well-documented technical and psychological phenomenon.

label: Online Radicalization
summary: Process by which individuals adopt increasingly extreme political, social, or religious views, often facilitated by online communities that provide validation, identity, and escalating content.

label: Conspiracy Theory Psychology
summary: Psychological and social dynamics underlying belief in conspiracy theories: need for cognitive closure, pattern recognition overactivation, distrust of institutions, social belonging in belief communities.

label: Satanic Panic
summary: 1980s-90s wave of false accusations of Satanic ritual abuse driven by moral panic, leading interview techniques, and false memories; produced wrongful convictions including the McMartin preschool case.

label: Antisemitism
summary: Prejudice against, hatred of, or discrimination against Jews; the oldest continuous form of bigotry in Western history, recurring across ideological contexts from medieval Christendom to modern far-right movements.

label: Toxic Masculinity
summary: Cultural norms of masculinity that harm both men and others: emotional suppression, dominance assertion, violence normalization, homophobia; linked to mental health crises in men, domestic violence, and mass shooting patterns.

label: Echo Chambers & Filter Bubbles
summary: Information environments where people encounter only views confirming their own beliefs; enabled by algorithmic personalization and self-selection; contributes to political polarization and makes viewpoint challenges psychologically alien.

label: Feminist Theory
summary: Theoretical framework analyzing gender oppression and its intersections with race, class, and sexuality; from liberal feminism (equal rights) to radical feminism (patriarchy as root oppression) to intersectional feminism (interlocking oppressions).

label: Intersectionality
summary: Framework by Kimberle Crenshaw analyzing how multiple social categories (race, gender, class, sexuality) overlap and interact to produce distinct experiences of privilege and oppression; cannot be understood through single-axis analysis.

label: Postmodernism
summary: Intellectual and cultural movement rejecting grand narratives, objective truth, and universal values. Influential in academia, art, and politics — critiqued for relativism and weaponized in anti-science discourse.

label: Social Media Radicalization
summary: Process by which individuals are drawn toward extreme ideologies through algorithmic recommendation systems, online communities, and targeted propaganda — accelerating political polarization and real-world violence.

label: Milgram Obedience Studies
summary: Stanley Milgram's 1961 experiments showing most ordinary people would administer apparently fatal electric shocks under authority pressure. Provided empirical foundation for understanding how ordinary people participate in atrocities.

label: Intergenerational Trauma
summary: The transmission of trauma and its psychological effects across generations through epigenetic changes, family dynamics, and cultural patterns. Documented in Holocaust survivors, Indigenous communities, and descendants of enslaved people.

label: Authoritarian Personality
summary: Adorno and colleagues' 1950 study identifying psychological profile predisposed to authoritarian obedience: rigid thinking, in-group conformity, submission to authority, and aggression toward out-groups — linked to fascist susceptibility.

label: Identity Politics
summary: Political mobilization based on shared identity — racial, gender, sexual, religious, or class — contested from both right (as divisive) and left (as essential for marginalized group representation). Central to culture war debates.

label: Post-Traumatic Growth
summary: The empirically documented phenomenon in which people experience significant positive psychological change following highly challenging life circumstances — not despite the trauma but through the process of struggling with it. Coined by Tedeschi and Calhoun (1996). Dimensions include: greater personal strength, new possibilities, relating to others more deeply, appreciation for life, and spiritual/existential change. Distinct from resilience (returning to baseline) — PTG involves surpassing the prior level of functioning. It does not invalidate trauma's harm; rather, it documents that human beings have a capacity for profound adaptation that oppressive systems cannot fully extinguish.

label: Moral Courage
summary: The psychological capacity to act on ethical principles in the face of social pressure, personal cost, or institutional opposition. Distinct from physical courage — it involves risking reputation, relationships, career, or safety for ethical reasons. Research identifies factors that enable it: strong moral identity, prior experience of principled action, social support from even one ally, and clear articulation of the ethical principle at stake. The mechanisms of atrocity — dehumanization, moral disengagement, obedience — are all defeated when sufficient individuals exercise moral courage. It is the individual-level mechanism that collective resistance depends on.

label: Bystander Intervention
summary: The willingness to intervene when witnessing harm to others, countering the bystander effect (Latané and Darley, 1968) in which responsibility diffuses in groups. Training programs demonstrate that the bystander effect is not fixed — knowing about it, seeing others intervene, and having rehearsed responses significantly increase intervention rates. Bystander intervention programs have shown effectiveness in reducing sexual assault, bullying, and hate crimes in institutional settings. At scale, bystander culture is the mechanism by which community norms against harm are enforced without formal authority.

label: Empathy
summary: The capacity to understand and share the feelings of others — to experience the world from another's perspective. Empathy is the psychological mechanism underlying moral circle expansion: extending moral consideration to previously excluded groups requires being able to imagine their suffering as real. It is activated by narrative (fiction reliably increases empathic understanding), contact (Allport's contact hypothesis), and social norms that affirm its expression. Dehumanization works by blocking empathy; literature, film, and cross-community contact work by activating it. Empathy's limitations include in-group bias and the tendency toward 'empathy fatigue' with distant or statistically described suffering.

label: Collective Resilience
summary: The capacity of communities to recover, adapt, and strengthen following disaster, trauma, or sustained oppression. Research on disasters, wartime communities, and post-conflict societies shows that collective resilience emerges from: pre-existing social networks, shared identity, effective leadership, and the perception of shared fate. Rebecca Solnit's 'A Paradise Built in Hell' documents how communities respond to disasters with mutual aid and solidarity rather than the chaos that authorities fear. Collective resilience is the community-level analog to post-traumatic growth — communities can emerge from catastrophe more cohesive than before.

label: Cognitive Behavioral Therapy (CBT)
summary: Evidence-based therapeutic approach developed by Aaron Beck that treats mental disorders by identifying and changing distorted thought patterns — the most studied psychotherapy in history.

label: Learned Helplessness
summary: Martin Seligman's discovery that organisms exposed to inescapable aversive events stop trying to escape even when escape becomes possible — a mechanism underlying depression and passivity in the face of oppression.

label: Growth Mindset
summary: Carol Dweck's research showing that people who believe abilities can be developed through effort outperform those who believe abilities are fixed, with significant implications for education.

label: Stereotype Threat
summary: Claude Steele's finding that awareness of a negative stereotype about one's group impairs performance in the stereotyped domain — a mechanism by which systemic racism and sexism depress outcomes beyond explicit discrimination.

label: Social Identity Theory
summary: Tajfel and Turner's theory that people's sense of self derives substantially from group memberships, explaining in-group favoritism, out-group discrimination, and the psychology of tribalism.

label: Humanistic Psychology
summary: Maslow and Rogers' reaction against behaviorism emphasizing human potential and self-actualization — the theoretical foundation of person-centered therapy.

label: Neuroplasticity
summary: The discovery that the adult brain can form new neural connections in response to learning and experience, providing biological basis for recovery, rehabilitation, and lifelong learning.

label: Implicit Bias Research (IAT)
summary: The Implicit Association Test revealed automatic unconscious associations that affect behavior independent of conscious beliefs, demonstrating that discrimination can operate without explicit racist intent.

label: Psychological Resilience
summary: Research showing that protective factors — secure attachment, community support, sense of purpose — predict recovery from extreme adversity, identifying the conditions that produce resilience.

label: Self-Determination Theory
summary: Deci and Ryan's framework holding that intrinsic motivation produces higher performance and wellbeing than extrinsic motivation, with implications for education, work design, and social policy.

label: Asch Conformity Experiments (1951)
summary: Solomon Asch's studies showing that most people give obviously incorrect answers when surrounded by confederates giving wrong answers — demonstrating the power of social pressure over individual perception.

label: Cognitive Dissonance Theory (1957)
summary: Leon Festinger's theory that discomfort from conflicting beliefs motivates attitude change — explaining how people rationalize bad behavior, resist correcting errors, and maintain false beliefs.

label: Terror Management Theory
summary: Theory that awareness of mortality drives much of human behavior — including group identification, worldview defense, and prejudice against those whose different beliefs threaten one's cultural anxiety buffer.

label: Just-World Hypothesis
summary: Melvin Lerner's finding that people need to believe the world is just and people get what they deserve — leading to victim-blaming and the rationalization of inequality as earned rather than structural.

label: Behaviorism & Positive Reinforcement
summary: B.F. Skinner's operant conditioning showed that behavior is shaped by its consequences; positive reinforcement produces more reliable and lasting behavior change than punishment — with applications in education, therapy, and social policy.
